content:
Hello,
I've followed the doc here to get make rusttest succeeding locally.
I'm hoping to start adding some rudimentary unit tests for the rust logic in the Android binder kernel driver here.

rusttest appears to only run tests under the rust subdirectory. I don't see any examples in the docs on how to enable tests like these elsewhere. Is it possible? Do I need to manually recreate what's going on in that Makefile, or is there some easier way?

Forgive me, I'm new :)

For the moment, I would suggest temporarily putting them under rust/ (if they are "pure" tests that can run in the host) or using doctests (if you need to use kernel APIs). The plan is to support a proper split of the rust/ folder, as well as allow for non-doctests to use kernel APIs.
Ok. To be clear, should one be able to write doctests outside of rust/, or do they also need to be in that directory (and the distinction is just having access to kernel APIs vs. not)?
rust/ only so far too, yeah (but that should be lifted too).
So eventually both should work with kernel APIs and anywhere in the tree (if I get my way... :)
You can also try José's https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/950

You might have issues with my PR as well, as described in the "possible issues/questions" section in the PR description:

As far as I know, procedural macros don't support $crate and I was not able to find a workaround to be able to use this macro both by the kernel crate and in drivers. In the macro, I use code like crate::bindings::<...> that won't work when used by a driver. Do you know if it is possible to fix this?

I didn't find a way to workaround this issue :S
You could use kernel::bindings::<...> unconditionally if you add extern crate self as kernel to rust/kernel/lib.rs.

FWIW - and another obligatory "sorry - I'm new" : )

I tried to add a new test under the rust/ directory, which works fine if you want to write "hello world". Then someone reminded me that I could import things by path, so I tried something like this:

#[path = "../drivers/android/rust_binder.rs"]
mod rust_binder;

But doing any of that leads to a whole host of compilation errors, as presumably the way things are wired together in rust/ does not match the compilation flags that the driver configures:

error[E0658]: use of unstable library feature 'allocator_api'
  --> rust/../drivers/android/thread.rs:56:16
   |
56 |     fn from(_: AllocError) -> Self {
   |                ^^^^^^^^^^
   |
   = note: see issue #32838 <https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/32838> for more information
   = help: add `#![feature(allocator_api)]` to the crate attributes to enable

error: aborting due to 286 previous errors; 1 warning emitted

Perhaps this is just a matter of getting something right in the Makefile. This is what I added to include my test source file:

# added to `rusttest` target
rusttest-android: private rustc_target_flags = --extern alloc \
    --extern build_error --extern macros --extern bindings
rusttest-android: $(src)/android.rs rusttest-prepare \
    rusttestlib-build_error rusttestlib-macros rusttestlib-bindings FORCE
    $(call if_changed,rustc_test)
    $(call if_changed,rustc_test_library)

Things around testing and the build system are going to change soon, but if you point me to your branch (or an example of test you would like to run), I can try to give you something that you can use meanwhile.

That would be awesome :)
The tldr is we'd like to address the TODOs in range_alloc.rs. As part of that it would be nice to write simple tests (for example that find_best_match actually returns the best match).
Thanks for the tip @bjorn3 , it works! I'll add the fix to v3 of the series. @Matt Gilbride with that change, the PR should be useful to you.

Thanks @José Expósito !
Perhaps I'm just being impatient, but since kunit is also new to me I figured I'd try things out. If you have an example you can point to where you are using this in the HID driver you mention, I'd be much obliged.

I'm assuming one or both are true:

  • adding extern crate self as kernel to rust/kernel/lib.rs isn't the only thing coming in v3 of your series to make it work
  • (much more likely) I don't know how to set my kunit test up properly

I've gone through the kunit docs a bit, leading to the test commit linked above. This command fails: tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig kunitconfig_rust_binder --arch=x86_64 with output like the following:

ERROR:root:make[4]: *** No rule to make target 'drivers/android/rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc.o', needed by 'drivers/android/built-in.a'.  Stop.
make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
error[E0433]: failed to resolve: could not find `kunit_test_suite` in the crate root
   --> ../drivers/android/range_alloc.rs:192:1
    |
192 | #[kunit_tests(rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc)]
    | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ could not find `kunit_test_suite` in the crate root
    |
    = note: this error originates in the attribute macro `kunit_tests` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)

Please don't hesitate to point me to a better forum for asking general kunit questions, but here goes:

  1. Is my config file kunitconfig_rust_binder sane? This is the result of make menuconfig - selecting all of the options to get various dependencies for rust and ANDROID_BINDER_IPC_RUST right, and then just appending these lines to the bottom:
# manually added kunit config
CONFIG_KUNIT=y
CONFIG_ANDROID_BINDER_IPC_RUST=y
CONFIG_BINDER_TEST=y
  1. What is the right target for the Makefile? The name passed to the macro? rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc.o? (this was a wild guess)
p.s. my branch here - forked from pull/950

Hi @Matt Gilbride,

I'm working on HID support on this branch. The first commit that introduces tests is this one. Have in mind that this is work in progress, so I'll rebase it many times.

In addition to that example, I added some documentation examples in the KUnit PR, hopefully they should help you as well.

About your error, it looks like you are missing some configuration options. Try to start with CONFIG_RUST=y and enable the missing options following the error output. Have in mind that every config that you add to your .config file to compile your driver should be added to your .kunitconfig.

Have in mind that every config that you add to your .config file to compile your driver should be added to your .kunitconfig

I believe I have all of the necessary options enabled - I checked in my config file for reference.

Have in mind that this is work in progress, so I'll rebase it many times. In addition to that example, I added some documentation examples in the KUnit PR, hopefully they should help you as well.

I am basing my branch off of yours here. Having pulled your most recent patch sets, I get a new error: 

ERROR:root:error[E0658]: mutation through a reference is not allowed in statics
   --> ../drivers/android/range_alloc.rs:192:1
    |
192 | #[kunit_tests(rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc)]
    | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

And if I attempt to add that feature to my own crate (to rust_binder.rs), it's not allowed

ERROR:root:error[E0725]: the feature `const_mut_refs` is not in the list of allowed features
 --> ../drivers/android/rust_binder.rs:8:12
  |
8 | #![feature(const_mut_refs)]
  |            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  • Maybe someone can help me figure out why the feature isn't allowed and/or how to configure things to allow it?
  • I notice that the code is all written underneath the rust/ directory in your HID branch. Should I be able to use this macro under drivers/android at this point?

Hi @Matt Gilbride ,

I notice that the code is all written underneath the rust/ directory in your HID branch. Should I be able to use this macro under drivers/android at this point?

Yes, you should be able to use it... If you manage to enable const_mut_refs. I don't know why you are not able to enable it, lets see if someone with more knowledge can help us to figure it out.
For code outside the rust/ folder, we only allow a certain set of unstable features. To add one, edit the variable rust_allowed_features in scripts/Makefile.build.

const_mut_refs still has an issue that is blocking stabilization. See the tracking issue: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/57349 If you avoid that specific code pattern, enabling it should be fine.

This worked for me! Given @bjorn3 's comment, are we open to making that change to scripts/Makefile.build at this time?

I do not need this urgently. I am happy to maintain my own patches allowing me to write unit tests in the binder driver for now.

Two quick follow-ups...

  1. Just curious how long it typically takes to get things like pull/950 merged?

  2. Forgive me if this is a general kunit question. Is it typical for a full build (as opposed to some sort of incremental one) to be triggered every time one runs, for example, this command: tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig kunitconfig_rust_binder --arch=x86_64

My machine always spends nearly 10 minutes building, even when I'm only changing doctest comments:

[20:58:56] Testing complete. Ran 98 tests: passed: 98
[20:58:56] Elapsed time: 604.980s total, 0.016s configuring, 577.963s building, 26.961s running

Perhaps there is something wrong with my kunit config? (I just ran menuconfig and then enabled the various things necessary for rust and rust kernel kunit tests). Or maybe this is just how it is at the moment?

Just curious how long it typically takes to get things like pull/950 merged?

We are not merging new features into the rust branch. We can make exceptions, but for Binder development you need some extra stuff in any case, no?

Is it typical for a full build (as opposed to some sort of incremental one) to be triggered every time one runs, for example, this command

I run the tests booting the kernel (since typically I test other things too), so I build the kernel normally (which of course works incrementally) -- not sure about the test runner approach. I have forwarded the question to David (see email).

My machine always spends nearly 10 minutes building, even when I'm only changing doctest comments:

Incremental builds should work, but I suggest trimming down your Kconfig in any case: you can get full builds that take e.g. less than a minute in a modern laptop.

Matt Gilbride said:

  1. Forgive me if this is a general kunit question. Is it typical for a full build (as opposed to some sort of incremental one) to be triggered every time one runs, for example, this command: tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig kunitconfig_rust_binder --arch=x86_64

My machine always spends nearly 10 minutes building, even when I'm only changing doctest comments:

[20:58:56] Testing complete. Ran 98 tests: passed: 98
[20:58:56] Elapsed time: 604.980s total, 0.016s configuring, 577.963s building, 26.961s running

Perhaps there is something wrong with my kunit config? (I just ran menuconfig and then enabled the various things necessary for rust and rust kernel kunit tests). Or maybe this is just how it is at the moment?

Yeah, this looks to be a problem with the kunitconfig: kunit.py definitely shouldn't always do a full rebuild. It looks like the kunitconfig file you're using is a full .config, when it really should only be a list of the few (non-default) options actually required for the test (and there dependencies).

It's probably worth taking a look at the KUnit docs, as well as @José Expósito's KUnit/Rust doc PR. As-is, this seems to work for me as a cut down version of your kunitconfig:

CONFIG_RUST=y
CONFIG_KUNIT=y
CONFIG_ANDROID_BINDER_IPC_RUST=y
CONFIG_BINDER_TEST=y
CONFIG_RUST_KERNEL_KUNIT_TEST=y

Which I can then run with:

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig kunitconfig_rust_binder --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_64

Which completes pretty quickly as a nothing-changed incremental build:

(snip)
[15:12:41] ==== rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc (1 subtest) ====
[15:12:41] [PASSED] test_hello_world
[15:12:41] ===== [PASSED] rust_android_binder_driver_range_alloc ======
[15:12:41] ============================================================
[15:12:41] Testing complete. Ran 103 tests: passed: 103
[15:12:41] Elapsed time: 16.367s total, 0.002s configuring, 12.232s building, 4.079s running

Thanks David! --make_options is the thing I missed in the docs, which sent me barking up the wrong tree resulting in that misguided config. Building the kernel when invoking kunit broke for me because I missed the LLVM=1.

Does it make any sense to add a section to https://docs.kernel.org/rust/quick-start.html about the kunit specifics? Something along these lines...

To run the rust kernel's kunit tests, create a minimal kunit config with the following:

CONFIG_RUST=y
CONFIG_KUNIT=y
CONFIG_RUST_KERNEL_KUNIT_TEST=y

And invoke kunit 

./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig ./path/to/above/.config --make_options LLVM=1 --arch x86_6

I was planning to add some notes on testing when I upstream the KUnit support (importantly, mentioning RUST_KERNEL_KUNIT_TEST which is the "new" option to be aware of). However, we avoid duplicating other docs in the Rust ones -- it is better to link instead.

I've been using @José Expósito 's changes to write tests for the android driver, and they've been working really well! I'm curious from reading https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#the-rust-subsystem - if/when these changes will be merged into the rust and/or rust-next branches?

We don't need this with any urgency, but would like to know if the changes are going to make it upstream before, say, end of year.

Matt Gilbride said:

I've been using José Expósito 's changes to write tests for the android driver, and they've been working really well! I'm curious from reading https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#the-rust-subsystem - if/when these changes will be merged into the rust and/or rust-next branches?

We don't need this with any urgency, but would like to know if the changes are going to make it upstream before, say, end of year.

I think there are a few (small tidy-up) changes still needed for those to land, but otherwise they look good from a KUnit point of view. So it's probably just waiting until those are fixed (and ideally someone with more Rust expertise than me has given it Reviewed-by).

From my reading of the website, there are no plans to add new features via the rust branch, so this will need to go via the normal upstream process. I'd be happy to take it via the kunit tree (assuming all of the dependencies are upstream and there are no unnecessary conflicts, which I'd have to check), otherwise I'd be fine with it going via rust-fixes.

Once it's accepted upstream, I imagine there'd be no problem backporting it to the rust branch if that were useful.
